entries.title: Thunderstorms arrive in the area Friday night before cooler temps Saturday
entries.summary: <p>Thunderstorms hit the area Friday night before cooler temperatures were expected for the weekend. </p><p>A tornado watch was in effect for 11 counties in Illinois until Friday at 11 p.m. Those counties iincluded: Cook, DuPage, Grundy, Kane, Kankakee, Kendall Lake, Will Livingston, Ford and Iroquois. A tornado warning was issued late Friday for the Kankakee area.</p><p>The National Weather Service cautions residents to "remain alert" amid the tornado watch. Tornadoes may develop rapidly from severe thunderstorms. If a tornado is spotted, residents are urged to go to a basement or a small central room in a sturdy structure, according to the weather service. </p><p>By Saturday, the high is expected be 30 degrees cooler than Friday's 80s, and by Saturday night temperatures could hover just above the freezing point, according to the weather service. A slight chance of rain is possible, and wind gusts are expected to reach 30 mph. </p><p>Conditions are expected to be calm Sunday and Monday, and highs in the 70s are expected to return by Tuesday. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: White Sox not sweating early struggles for closer Seranthony Dominguez
entries.summary: <p>WEST SACRAMENTO, Calif. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> general manager <b>Chris Getz</b> signed closer <b>Seranthony Dominguez</b> to a two-year, $20 million <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/01/23/white-sox-to-sign-reliever-seranthony-dominguez" target="_blank" ><u>deal</u></a> to lock down exactly the kind of situation he walked into Thursday against Tampa Bay.</p><p>Staked to a 3-2 ninth-inning lead and facing the heart of the order, Dominguez promptly served up a game-tying home run to Junior Caminero, then gave up a single, walk and a wild pitch before hitting a batter and getting the hook en route to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-head-west-with-that-familiar-losing-feeling-rays" target="_blank" ><u>a deflating 5-3 loss</u></a>.</p><p>Dominguez served up a crunch-time homer to <b>Christian Yelich </b>on opening weekend in Milwaukee, too, and his ERA ballooned to 7.50 following his second blown save.</p><p>But don’t call it buyer’s remorse just yet.</p><p>“It was an outing in which he wasn't at his best, certainly. But our confidence in him is not going to change just because of one outing,” manager <b>Will Venable</b> said Friday of Dominguez, who has been sharp in most of his other five outings. “We still have confidence that he can go out there and help us win games at the back end of our bullpen.”</p><h3><b>Not o-Kay?</b></h3><p>Sox left-hander <b>Anthony Kay</b> wasn’t thrilled to find out “like 10 minutes before” Thursday’s game that he’d be throwing in bulk, facing the sixth Rays hitter behind opener <b>Jordan Leasure</b>.</p><p>“It’s not really ideal,” said Kay, who signed a two-year, $12 million deal in the offseason <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/12/09/anthony-kay-regained-his-confidence-overseas-white-sox-hope-he-can-translate-that-success-to-the-majors" target="_blank" ><u>after two years playing in Japan</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-right> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6e0000" name="image-6e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium-3x4"> <source type="image/webp" width="375" height="500" alt="Anthony Kay kicks the mound after Tampa Bay Rays' Taylor Walls hit a one-run single during the fourth inning Thursday." width="375" height="500" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b930633/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2567x3423+0+0/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fae%2F39%2F0ac6c0d54fd0be6a420e7a6401ba%2Frays-white-sox-baseball.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anthony Kay kicks the mound after Tampa Bay Rays’ Taylor Walls hit a one-run single during the fourth inning Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Nam Y. Huh/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Following <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/09/white-sox-royals-kauffman-stadium-anthony-kay-noah-schultz" target="_blank" ><u>a strong start</u></a> in Kansas City (5 ⅔ scoreless innings with six K’s, two walks and no opener), Kay went 2 ⅔ innings, giving up a run on four hits with a walk and a strikeout. But he wasn’t making excuses: “My job is to get outs, regardless of what situation it is."</p><p>Venable said Friday that “if that was something that he wasn’t pleased about, that’s something certainly that we’ll iron out.”</p><p>“Sometimes in asking guys to do this, it’s not going to be perfect… and you’ve got to go out there and do your job regardless. I’m definitely sensitive, especially as we’re asking guys to do things that might take them out of their routine, to make sure that they have the information that they need when they need it,” Venable said. “So if there was something we can do better on our end, we’ll certainly fix it.”</p><p>Sox hurlers have seemed less than enthusiastic about Venable’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>use of openers</u></a> five times so far this season, four by <b>Grant Taylor</b>. The second-year manager he’s deploying the strategy to create more favorable matchups, and “I’m always going to do what I think is best for the team.”</p><p>“We’re all doing this in the spirit of trying to find ways to win, and I think every single one of these guys is behind that idea and supports that. I’ve been transparent about that from Day 1,” Venable said.</p><h3><b>Coming and going</b></h3><p>Before opening their series against the Athletics, the Sox called up left-hander <b>Doug Nikhazy</b> from Triple-A Charlotte and sent down reliever <b>Tyler Gilbert</b>.</p><p>The Sox claimed Nikhazy, 26, off waivers earlier this month from Cleveland, where he struggled in one of his first two big-league games last year. In a five-inning start and a four-inning relief appearance for the Knights, Nikhazy struck out 10, walked three and gave up three runs.</p><p>“He's here to give us some length” against a lefty-heavy Athletics lineup, Venable said.</p><p>Gilbert, who tossed a no-hitter for Arizona in 2021 and drew umpires’ scrutiny last year for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/09/26/tyler-gilbert-glove-sticky-mlb-confiscate" target="_blank" ><u>a sticky mitt</u></a> later deemed legal, was touched up for six earned runs in two Sox appearances spanning 2 ⅔ innings while shuttling back and forth twice from Charlotte.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Practice makes prospect for Montini QB Israel Abrams
entries.summary: <p>When you see Montini’s Israel Abrams throw a tight spiral 30 yards across the field to a receiver in stride, it seems pretty effortless.</p><p>But Broncos coach Mike Bukovsky says Abrams makes it look easy thanks to countless hours of work.</p><p>“The thing I will tell everybody [is] he loves to practice,” Bukovsky told the Sun-Times. “There’s people you coach who don’t love to practice. He loves to work on his craft; he loves to throw. It’s the backbone of his success.”</p><p>And it has made Abrams one of the most sought-after players in the 2027 recruiting cycle. The 6-4, 187-pounder is a consensus five-star prospect, ranked the No. 2 quarterback and No. 39 player overall nationally.</p><p>He committed to 2025 CFP runner-up Miami this month, picking the Hurricanes over national champ Indiana, Ole Miss and Tennessee.</p><p>“Me and my family got down there and [saw] what it was all about — the culture and all the coaches and how I’ll be coached,” Abrams told the Sun-Times. “It was a good vibe.”</p><p>National recruiting analyst Clint Cosgrove loves Abrams’ upside in such a high-powered offense.</p><p>“His arm talent is so impressive,” Cosgrove told the Sun-Times. “He just reads everything so well, so quick and gets the ball out so fast.”</p><p>“I’ve been coaching high school football for 36 years, and I’ve seen a couple of kids that maybe approached his arm strength,” Bukovsky said. “What I’ve never seen is his ability to navigate in the pocket.”</p><p>Again, it was something Abrams worked at.</p><p>“Most definitely, that was my main focus sophomore [year] into junior season,” he said.</p><p>The effort paid off.</p><p>Abrams led the Broncos to a 14-0 record and the Class 4A title last fall, completing 69% of his passes for 4,072 yards and 40 touchdowns with six interceptions. He also ran for 224 yards and 10 touchdowns.</p><p>But gaudy as they were, the stats weren’t something Abrams was preoccupied with.</p><p>“He’s never talked to me about numbers,” Bukovsky said. “He totally understands the team concept.”</p><h3><b>Passarella departs DePaul</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Michael Passarella, who coached DePaul Prep to the Class 4A state title in 2024, resigned after seven seasons to become the defensive coordinator at Elmhurst University.</p><p>Passarella and Elmhurst coach Mike Heffernan were college teammates at Illinois State and have known each other for two decades.</p><p>“My idea, long term, I wanted to be a head football coach in college,” Passarella told the Sun-Times.</p><p>For family reasons, he wanted to stay in the Chicago area while moving toward that goal.</p><p>“It had to make sense in terms of philosophy and schedule,” Passarella said.</p><p>He took over a DePaul team that was 0-10 the year before he arrived, coming off a winless regular season and a Catholic League playoff loss. His record with the Rams was 28-38.</p><p>Though the 2024 title run was the Rams’ only playoff appearance under Passarella, the program’s numbers are up, and he believes it is positioned for success.</p><p>“We were young, and there is talent there,” he said. “There are a handful of kids who are Division I prospects. That place could be a real gold mine.”<br></p>
entries.author: Mike Clark

entries.title: The Sky are new, but I'm not sure they are improved
entries.summary: <p>He used the words ‘‘put a plan together,’’ ‘‘we needed to improve dramatically’’ and ‘‘we wanted a new identity,’’ all damn near in the same sentence. Letting the world — or the very chosen few who cared to listen or cared at all — know that this new was going to be different from the last new.</p><p>And the other new he inherited when he got here.</p><p>Sky general manager Jeff Pagliocca also went on to say, in this first week of the post-Angel Reese life of the team he blew up (for the second time in his three-year tenure), only to piece back together again: ‘‘We landed our targets.’’</p><p>Dope. Now the target — mainly because of their trade of Reese — is openly on them.</p><p>Finally the talking points, corporate-speak and daily media-generated filibustering have come to an end. The here is now. And this iteration of this new version — and ‘‘new-look,’’ as some have poured into calling them — of the Sky in unrivaled theory has stepped forth with hope that we believe their sale of ‘‘things will no longer be the same.’’</p><p>It’s a hard sell, one many of the franchises in Chicago have been gaslighting their fans — both sustained and lost — with to lure us into their pretense that way more often than not turns out to be false. But with those others, their new has been incremental, a slow burn to resurrection. The Sky? This is GLP-1 on Ozempic.</p><p>Getting ‘‘All Gas’’ Gabriela Jaquez with the No. 5 overall pick; getting Azura Stevens back; getting Skylar Diggins and Rickea Jackson; getting Ajsa Sivka (the No.10 overall pick last year who decided to stay and finish high school and further develop in the Spanish league) to come to America to hoop; getting ‘‘Sloot’’ (the great Courtney Vandersloot) back after she missed practically the whole season last season because of her ACL tear — nothing’s going to be the same. Which is the problem.</p><p>Problem being, if things are going to be the same for the other teams — specifically in the Eastern Conference — the Sky have to ascend above just being new and different for all of the changes made to make sense. Because even as things aren’t at all the same here but remain primarily the same everywhere else, the Sky still have ... problems.</p><p>The Dream, 16 games above .500 last season, one of only three teams last season to win 30 games before losing to the Fever (without Caitlin Clark) in a final Game 3 by two points, added Reese to their bona fides. Their same — for the most part, even without adding Angel — is better than the Sky’s better.</p><p>The Liberty, champs two seasons ago, finished 10 games above .500 last season and by adding Satou Sabally became the possible front-runners with the Aces to own the 2026 chip. Their same and better is better than the Sky’s better.</p><p>The Mystics, who finished six games better than the Sky in the regular season last season and just added three major first-round rookies, including Lauren Betts and Cotie McMahon, along with Rori Harmon in the second round of the draft to go with the two rookies they drafted last season (Kiki Iriafen and Sonia Citron), both of whom were named All-Stars, yeah, their better is more of an upgrade than the Sky’s better.</p><p>Anoint the Fever being conference finalists last season. Again, mostly without CC22 on the floor. Simply look at the top of their rotation, then look at the Sky’s new one. The Fever’s same — especially with the return of a healthy CC22 — is arguably better than the Sky’s better. With proof that they can win together.</p><p>And depending on what happens with the Sun — who, by the way, finished last season one game ahead of the Sky — in their final season in Connecticut, the Sky are still looking on paper and screen at being on the low end (fifth or sixth place) in a seven-team conference race to finish better than they have three seasons and counting.</p><p>Mountains.</p><p>Recently, someone outside of Chicago made a claim: ‘‘The worst GM in all of sports, his name is Jeff Pagliocca, and he’s the Chicago Sky GM’’ is what DC radio host Chuck Montiano claimed on KFPA 94.1FM’s ‘‘The Collision.’’ Hard to accept when, in the same city, during the same time, we’ve had AKME (Bulls), Kyle Davidson (Blackhawks) and Chris Getz (White Sox).</p><p>But it verifies there’s a target even outside of Chi on Pagliocca and the Sky franchise to outperform their new presentation of new. To imitate what the Bears did last season (without stealing the wonderkid head coach from every other team in the league; without the QB prodigy) with prayers that two or three teams above them do what the Lions and Vikings did: fall.</p><p>Then hope the new ‘‘new’’ lasts.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: Horoscope for Saturday, April 18, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Taurus.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a favorable day with finances, cash flow, shopping and dealing with your possessions. Respect your moneymaking ideas. You might purchase something that pleases you. Meanwhile, today you're attracted to mysteries as well as psychology, astrology and the occult. You want to know stuff.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a lovely day for you because the moon is in your sign lined up with your ruler Venus and dancing with lucky, moneybags Jupiter. This makes you happy, cheerful and successful in dealing with others, especially groups. It's a great day to party! It's also a great day to deal with kids' activities and make plans for your kids. Co-workers are helpful.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a work-hard/party-hard day! With your strong focus on home and family, intense discussions might take place that reveal some truths or facts that are new to you. It's also a lovely day to socialize. Romance is blessed. Enjoy your interactions with others today.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, conversations with others will be enlightening. Your mind will be curious today, making it easier to penetrate secrets or learn hidden truths. This is also a good day to make future plans. Entertain at home or explore real estate opportunities.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your insight might see ways to boost your income today or to make a little money on the side. This is also an excellent day to make plans about how to earn money in the future. Meanwhile, your sense of optimism will attract others to you and lift their spirits, as well.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>TV host, actor and comedian Conan O'Brien (1963) shares your birthday today. You are idealistic, warmhearted and practical. You value tradition and justice, and you champion the causes of the downtrodden. Exciting changes will bring you increased personal freedom. Learn to be more flexible. Let go of what holds you back. Seek out new opportunities and be ready to act fast. Expect to travel.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Munetaka Murakami adjusting to MLB pitching along with anemic White Sox offense
entries.summary: <p>WEST SACRAMENTO, Calif. — After a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/munetaka-murakami-chicago-white-sox-debut" target="_blank" ><u>boisterous introduction</u></a> to Major League Baseball with three home runs in his first three games, the hits haven’t been easy to come by for Munetaka Murakami.</p><p>The slugger dubbed the Babe Ruth of Higo, Japan, has looked more like the Adam Dunn of yore with his three-true-outcome propensity, a big swing-and-miss profile the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> knew and embraced when it brought him down to their price range for a two-year, $34 million deal.</p><p>But to revive one of baseball’s worst offenses, the Sox will need some more positive outcomes from Murakami beyond the strikeouts, walks and dingers their 26-year-old first baseman has piled up in the early going. Murakami says he knows he can get there as he adjusts in kind to the big-league pitchers adjusting to his prodigious bat.</p><p>“The pitchers have come out with a different variety of pitches obviously from the first series and now. But again, it’s about getting used to it,” Murakami said <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/14/noah-schultz-white-sox-mlb-debut" target="_blank" ><u>earlier this week</u></a> via interpreter Kenzo Yagi, after smacking his first homer in 10 days to break a 1-for-25 skid at the plate. “I’m seeing a lot of pitches right now. Getting used to it and getting that preparation is my first priority.”</p><p>As the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-head-west-with-that-familiar-losing-feeling-rays" >Sox headed west</a> to open a three-game series against the Athletics, Murakami carried a lopsided .167/.346/.417 line over his first 19 career games, with five homers and five singles in 60 at-bats against a whopping 26 strikeouts and 17 walks. He was 3-for-5 Friday with a grand slam, two singles and two strikeouts in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/17/white-sox-seranthony-dominguez-anthony-kay-athletics" >Sox’ 9-2 victory</a>.</p><p>He’s still buoying a cellar-dwelling Sox offense that was collectively below the Mendoza line (.195) to go with another MLB-worst OPS of .602.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ea0001" name="image-ea0001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eed1be2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/31f6dcc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Munetaka Murakami takes batting practice earlier this week at Rate Field." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27d4ba8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ec5df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3107x1744+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F9e%2Fb537fc5e42fbbdff093810711f9f%2F2271388937.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Munetaka Murakami takes batting practice earlier this week at Rate Field.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Michael Reaves/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While Murakami has shown elite figures through mid-April in bat speed (74.1 mph), average exit velocity (93.7 mph) and barrel percentage (22.9%), his whiff (41.3%) and strikeout (33.3%) rates were among the highest in MLB, according to Baseball Savant.</p><p>And while opponents’ initial scouting reports had them going after Murakami with high heat, they’ve swiftly zeroed in on a tendency to chase offspeed and breaking pitches — more than half of which Murakami has waved on.</p><p>But he says he’s fine-tuning his own response by putting in time with the Sox’ Trajekt machine, an artificial intelligence-powered system that can replicate any pitcher for batting practice.</p><p>“I was kind of letting the easy pitches pass by me, the breaking balls in early stages. But now I’m seeing the ball a lot better and I’m really getting into the plate with a lot more confidence,” Murakami said. “The routine really hasn’t changed that much. Obviously if a pitcher that is new is coming up, I will go in Trajekt and see a lot of pitches from them.”</p><p>Sox hitting director Ryan Fuller said he expects Murakami to “dial it back a little bit, see the ball a little bit deeper, use the whole field.”</p><p>“His ability to make adjustments at a really high level has been very impressive and you can see it. His swings are starting to be a little bit more fluid, finding the barrel a little bit more,” Fuller said.</p><p>General manager Chris Getz said he’s not worried about his big offseason splash.</p><p>“He is going through an adjustment period with catching up to some fastballs,” Getz said. “We know what he's capable of doing. The great thing is he's already shown [it] and I know that he believes in himself. Getting back into a home routine I think is going to serve him well."</p><p>The Sox’ Trajekt stays at Rate Field, but a little more time to settle into his new city and an entirely new country could serve Murakami well, too. He got to take in his first Blackhawks game in a box at the United Center with his teammates on Monday, his first Chicago off-day.</p><p>“It was really exciting and fun,” Murakami said. “It’s a grind, I’m going home, hotel, facilities and then a routine of that daily. I really haven’t gotten into the fun part of enjoying Chicago yet, but it will come.”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: There’s no wagering on pro wrestling — but who knows what the future could bring?
entries.summary: <p>LAS VEGAS — Clay Baker envisioned Hulk Hogan lifting the 520-pound Andre the Giant and slamming him to the Pontiac Silverdome canvas, and he still can’t quite grasp those dynamics.</p><p>“It happened faster than imagined because I didn’t expect Andre to be slammed,” Baker said. “It hadn’t been done before. King Kong Bundy and Big John Studd tried and failed.</p><p>“Since the whole crowd was standing, it felt like everybody imperceptibly swayed up and then down, just like the action in the ring.”</p><p>Baker, 52, has spent 30 years in the Las Vegas sports-talk radio industry. He hosts a morning Raiders show and co-hosts JT the Brick’s afternoon session.</p><p>In March 1987, Baker was 13 when he attended the third WrestleMania with his mother, Barbara Ann. Had sports betting been legal in Michigan, and had rasslin’ been on the menu, he would have given her a fiver to bet on Andre.</p><p>He would have lost that fin.</p><p>Eighty thousand people had packed the big barn; some estimates hovered around 93,000. Baker harkened back to that scene and called it a bookmaker’s dream.</p><p>“Half were on Andre, the other half on Hulk,” he said. “Everybody loved it so much, it would have been the greatest handle of all time. It was the most friendly, most enjoyable event.</p><p>“I was used to going to the Silverdome and seeing fights, all sorts of bad things. But my mom and I sat there and laughed, for nine hours. It was wonderful, people hugging each other and laughing the whole time.”</p><p>The card captivated young Clay.</p><p>“Honky Tonk Man slammed his guitar onto Jake Roberts’ head. I missed Mr. Fuji with his cane, but Kabuki threw his trademark green dust in faces.”</p><p>Baker laughed. Barbara Ann had been raised in rural West Virginia, and in the early 1950s, she’d glare through a window at the wrestling on the TV set inside a neighbor’s home.</p><p>“Wrestling was all that would be on during the day,” Clay said. “She and a cousin, Terry, loved French-Canadian wrestler Johnny Rougeau, and they joined his fan club.</p><p>“At WrestleMania III, Johnny’s son lost to Greg Valentine and Brutus Beefcake, amid controversy. And my stately mother stood on her chair and booed loudly!”</p><h3>Quick Vegas return</h3><p>So why isn’t it legal to bet on wrestling?</p><p>From an Elimination Chamber on Feb. 28 at the United Center, some victors advanced here to WrestleMania 42 at Allegiant Stadium, staged Saturday and Sunday.</p><p>SmackDown took place Friday at T-Mobile Arena.</p><p>One year ago, the two-day event at Allegiant drew 118,641. A share of TKO Group Holdings, Inc., parent company of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), has hovered around $200 since Christmas, an 18-month value doubling.</p><p>Its popularity is undeniable.</p><p>“This is one of the best wrestling events of all time,” Baker said, “with a whole week of events leading up to it. Older wrestlers like Ric Flair see this as not only about making money, but reconnecting with others.</p><p>“A lot of people in the wrestling community were so vocal about it, that’s why Vegas got it again, and it might be here a lot more frequently.”</p><p>It hits Italy in May, and Saudi Arabia plays host to WrestleMania 43 next year.</p><p>In his lifetime, Baker believes it will be legal to wager on wrestling.</p><p>However, he sees it requiring someone with the integrity and tact of Marc Ratner, the former executive director of the state’s athletic commission who was vital in getting UFC sanctioned in New York, and elsewhere, to trumpet it.</p><p>“Local elections, too,” Baker said. “We’ll see saturation and fatigue with normal pro sports, then they’ll try something like that. You’re damn right we should be able to bet on it!”</p><h3>Proper props</h3><p>So, it’s scripted.</p><p>Baker admitted, “When you’re pre-18, you take this [bleep] seriously. Post-18? It’s, Awww, I shoulda known!” He laughed. “But there would be [betting] limits.”</p><p>This is topical, with the NFL Draft starting Thursday. Some books wisely have slim draft menus, others are deep. At William Hill, defensive lineman is -150 to be the first position picked by the Bears, safety +330, 11-to-1 for linebacker.</p><p>To a degree, that’s scripted, too. Plus, -Super Bowl menus are ridiculous. Think someone didn’t know in advance that Sea-hawks coach Mike Macdonald would be victory-doused with lime-colored Gatorade?</p><p>That was +260, or risk $100 to win $260, at William Hill, behind blue (+200) and orange (+250).</p><p>Moreover, Bad Bunny’s halftime songs had prices.</p><p>“There were bets on the first song he’d sing at this past Super Bowl’s halftime,” Westgate SuperBook executive consultant Jay Kornegay said, “with thousands of people around the rehearsal?”</p><p>Kiosks and tellers would have done brisk Silverdome betting business in 1987, but those indulgent patrons wouldn’t have bothered Kornegay.</p><p>“I’m not worried about the fans,” Kornegay said. “I would be worried about the non-fans [sharp punters] seeking an edge.”</p><h3>Offshore only?</h3><p>Ultimately, what if rasslers practiced various endings, the actual clincher remaining unknown until the bout began? What if non-disclosure forms were signed?</p><p>“Still,” Kornegay said, “I would be concerned.”</p><p>Kornegay knows how the state’s Gaming Control Board, which gauges and green-lights potential sportsbook wagers, might react to a request to post WWE props.</p><p>“They would do their due diligence and find out what this is,” he said. “Some might think it’s just a wrestling match. Others would say, ‘These are pre-rehearsed, predetermined events.’</p><p>“And once they discovered that, they’d ban those type of wagers on something that’s predetermined.”</p><p>Veteran oddsman Nick Bogdanovich echoed that line to Bookies.com scribe Brent James.</p><p>“But I do know that the faraway places that don’t answer to regulators, they would get away with it, and they have,” Bogdanovich said. “They book WrestleMania and SummerSlam, all of those events.</p><p>“That’s one [Nevada] would not be able to pull off.”</p><p>One day, however, some state’s regulatory body might proceed differently.</p><h3>Beers with the brain</h3><p>In our youth on Saturdays, the older brother and I clowned on televised wrestling. We always thought Pompero Firpo, the jungle wildman, was called Pompero “Purple.”</p><p>On March 4, 1987, I ventured early to the San Diego Sports Arena, with San Diego State fraternity brothers, for a WWF event to maybe find some luminaries hanging poolside at the nearby motel.</p><p>We encountered Chicago native Bobby “The Brain” Heenan, the ace promoter and former rassler, at a metal table. We settled beside him and drained beers as he regaled us with fantastic tales.</p><p>In huge swim trunks, Andre the Giant either reclined on side-by-side lounge chairs or sat in the Jacuzzi. The British Bulldogs frolicked in the pool.</p><p>Upon returning to the Sig Ep house, 10 of us engaged in a chapter-room Battle Royale, last one remaining wins.</p><p>Three hog-tied my 6-5 frame and tossed me through an open window out onto the asphalt parking lot.</p><p>The cuts, bruises and blood sure seemed real.<br></p>
entries.author: Rob Miech

entries.title: Ahmed Shihab-Eldin excels at exposing injustice. His overseas arrest deserves the same scrutiny.
entries.summary: <p>Our conversation didn't last long.</p><p>The cheers would have drowned out our small talk, and <a class="Link" href="https://www.ahmedshihabeldin.com/" target="_blank" >Ahmed Shihab-Eldin</a> and I had to tend to the <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/rummanahussain/status/669600585822400512?s=20" target="_blank" >crowd </a>that swarmed <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/rummanahussain/status/669604262767370240?s=20" target="_blank" >Malcolm London</a>, a young poet and activist who had been cleared of punching a Chicago police officer during protests decrying the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/10/5/18422864/16-shots-a-guilty-verdict-and-a-chicago-cop-goes-to-jail-for-killing-a-teen" target="_blank" >deadly police shooting</a> of Laquan McDonald.</p><p>Soon after I told Shihab-Eldin I admired his work while we reported on Cook County prosecutors' decision to drop charges against London, the award-winning journalist followed me back on Twitter.</p><p>I received another notification — less friendly than Shihab-Eldin's gesture — on my social media feed that same day over 10 years ago. A woman who trekked to the Leighton Criminal Courthouse with dozens of others to support London scolded me for posting her picture in my news story.</p><p>She didn't object to my using the snapshot over safety concerns. Rather, she was irate that I didn't ask for permission, which as a media professional <a class="Link" href="https://www.quillmag.com/2020/06/04/ethics-should-journalists-take-photos-and-video-of-protesters/" target="_blank" >I am not required to do</a> when covering demonstrations and rallies in public spaces. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-360000" name="module-360000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>I was lucky I only got a lecture.</p><p>Shihab-Eldin, 41, is now facing more <a class="Link" href="https://cpj.org/2026/04/cpj-launches-petition-calling-on-kuwait-to-release-us-kuwaiti-journalist-ahmed-shihab-eldin/" target="_blank" >dire consequences</a> for doing his job.</p><p>The Berkeley, California, native is believed to have been detained in Kuwait for nearly seven weeks, not for disseminating what was unfolding on the date palm-lined sidewalks, but apparently for sharing footage and the aftermath of activity miles above in the sky.</p><p>The images Shihab-Eldin reposted on Substack and social media since the United States and Israel launched airstrikes in Iran in late February were "vetted and verified" and used by reputable news outlets, according to <a class="Link" href="https://cpj.org/about/people/#gypsy-guillen-kaiser" target="_blank" >Gypsy Guillén Kaiser</a>, the chief global affairs officer for the Committee to Protect Journalists. </p><p>None of the videos posted were filmed by Shihab-Eldin, a dual Kuwaiti American national who has worked at PBS, The New York Times, Al Jazeera English, The Huffington Post and "Vice" on HBO, Guillén Kaiser said. </p><p>Kuwaiti authorities have yet to officially confirm what Shihab-Eldin has been accused of. But through their research, CPJ staffers were able to piece together that he was likely locked up and <a class="Link" href="https://cpj.org/2026/04/cpj-calls-on-kuwait-to-release-us-kuwaiti-journalist-ahmed-shihab-eldin/" target="_blank" >charged</a> with breaking a relatively vague law designed to curtail press freedom under the guise of "harming national security."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/248b117/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5b74da0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e80a719/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A black and white headshot of a man." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e80a719/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fff967/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e80a719/2147483647/strip/true/crop/959x538+0+210/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6d%2F5eede72b46619550d70f53870353%2Fimg-0394.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ahmed Shihab-Eldin</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Shihab-Eldin was last heard from on March 2 — the date of his <a class="Link" href="https://news.sky.com/story/journalist-detained-in-kuwait-after-posting-iran-war-video-activists-say-13532010" target="_blank" >last post</a>: a <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/14/media/ahmed-shihab-eldin-kuwait-iran-war-detained" target="_blank" >CNN clip</a> of a U.S. Air Force fighter jet crash over Kuwait, where Shihab-Eldin had been visiting relatives. The same day, the country's interior ministry <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/MOInformation/status/2028608058750865618?s=20" target="_blank" >warned</a> against filming or publishing videos or information tied to the Iran war and relayed that <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/CPJMENA/status/2044836936058065193?s=20" target="_blank" >several people</a> were arrested for spreading false news. Less than two weeks later, the U.S. ally issued a <a class="Link" href="https://kuwaittimes.com/article/41128/kuwait/other-news/10-years-jail-for-undermining-military-services/" target="_blank" >decree stipulating</a> a sentence of up to 10 years in prison for such offenses that "undermine the prestige of the military or deliberately work toward eroding public trust in them."</p><p>"It's a tragedy," Guillén Kaiser said of Shihab-Eldin's detention in a country that lists "freedom of opinion and scientific research" in its <a class="Link" href="https://www.kna.kw/PdfHelper?FilePath=rpS1JRuUjqyzPmmqQDsTPuyX-OkNYd0Ca6EM3rzn9jtWR_xaCn6-aFFTCuVKQAUs7LAl-iDuIzEYiFZRiS7mz9UMCkmdMoC3iSE0qy2hK2NwV7HoFNYFiy48jQk77xTp" target="_blank" >constitution</a> and is among the signatories of the <a class="Link" href="https://treaties.un.org/pages/viewdetails.aspx?chapter=4&clang=_en&mtdsg_no=iv-4&src=ind#:~:text=Depositary,782.2001." target="_blank" >United Nations’ International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights</a>.</p><p>Guillén Kaiser hopes and expects the Trump administration will "vigorously pursue" Shihab-Eldin's release.</p><p>His sisters, Lara and Luma Eldin, expressed the same sentiment in statements they and their international legal team issued Friday. </p><p>I hope they are right. </p><p>President Donald Trump hasn't exactly been quick to protect most journalists' rights, and many of our elected leaders have established that the well-being of Palestinians and the Palestinian diaspora isn't their priority.</p><p>Shihab-Eldin, whose parents were forced to flee to Kuwait from their native Palestine before immigrating to the U.S., continually travels the world to do on-the-ground reports that serve as an antidote to such discriminatory attitudes, his friends said.</p><p>"Ahmed has consistently centered and amplified communities that are too often underrepresented or excluded from mainstream narratives," said <a class="Link" href="https://www.wsj.com/news/author/sabrina-siddiqui" target="_blank" >Sabrina Siddiqui</a>, the national politics reporter for the Wall Street Journal who worked with Shihab-Eldin at the Huffington Post.</p><p>Shihab-Eldin has often used his trademark thoughtfulness to think outside the box, added Sree Sreenivasan, the co-founder of the <a class="Link" href="https://saja.org/" target="_blank" >South Asian Journalists Association</a> and a former professor at Columbia University's Graduate School of Journalism.</p><p>"When he was my <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/sree/status/2044059463523488233?s=20" target="_blank" >student</a> around 2006-2007, he was exploring the possibilities and opportunities for audience engagement and digital innovations way before we even had the terminology for those things," Sreenivasan said.</p><p>Sreenivasan, Siddiqui and others in Shihab-Eldin's orbit didn't learn about his detention until the news broke to the rest of the public last week.</p><p>"I'm sure he knew of the dangers involved. But no one expected this," Sreenivasan said.</p><p>When Shihab-Eldin and I crossed paths in Chicago more than a decade ago, the judge presiding over the case we were covering told the man standing before her, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/rummanahussain/status/669599516564332544?s=20" target="_blank" >“You are free to go.”</a></p><p>Many of us want Shihab-Eldin to hear those same words too.</p><p><i>Rummana Hussain is a columnist and leads the opinion coverage at the Sun-Times.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-280000" name="module-280000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Rummana Hussain

entries.title: Most Illinoisans OK with data centers — as long they're strictly regulated
entries.summary: <p>In a mid-March poll, 68% of likely Illinois voters said they would support legislation to “regulate data centers to minimize their impact on our utility bills, climate, and water while still allowing them to be built.”</p><p>But while 21% opposed the legislation, more than half of those opponents (56%) said they did so because they “oppose allowing data centers to be built at all.” That means 80% either want guardrails or oppose any new construction.</p><p>So, yeah, it’s not looking great for anyone who might want to fend off regulation. As you’ll see below, this is an 80-plus % issue pretty much across the board.</p><p>Global Strategy Group conducted the survey of 800 likely voters March 16-19 on behalf of the Illinois Clean Jobs Coalition, which is pushing the Protecting Our Water, Energy, and Ratepayers, or POWER, Act, legislation (<a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=4016&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&SessionID=114&GA=104" target="_blank" >Senate Bill 4016</a>/<a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?GAID=18&DocNum=5513&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=0&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >House Bill 5513</a>). The survey had a margin of error of plus or minus 3.5%.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e80000" name="module-e80000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>The proposal was initially supported by large bipartisan majorities, the poll found, as well as in all regions of the state. Democrats and independents supported it the most (71%), while Republicans support was strong, but not by as much at 61%.</p><p>And 56% of Republicans who said they opposed the bill told the pollster they did so because they didn’t want any data centers built, period. The same was said by 64% of likely downstate voters. Keep in mind that the margin of error for voter subsets is substantially higher.</p><p>Organized labor has asked the governor to not pursue his proposed two-year moratorium on state data center construction tax breaks. The governor stood by his proposal, but did offer the possibility of a compromise.</p><p>Whether the environmentalists' bill is the answer remains to be seen. But it seems clear from the poll that Illinoisans want some real regulation of this industry.</p><p>For example, 96% said they agreed that the state should “ensure data centers pay their fair share of water infrastructure costs,” with 85% strongly agreeing. Ninety-three percent say data centers should be required to “use efficient cooling systems to reduce water consumption,” with 75% strongly agreeing. You almost never see poll numbers like that on anything.</p><p>Ninety-one percent said they want to “prohibit data centers from shifting their energy costs to consumer bills,” and 91% also said they want to “require data centers to provide their own clean energy and storage to power their operations.” The only difference was intensity. Eighty-four percent strongly agreed that cost-shifting should be outlawed, while 70% strongly support requiring the centers to provide their own clean energy and storage.</p><p>It goes on from there. “Require an analysis that a data center won't increase harmful health, water, and environmental impacts on vulnerable communities before a project moves forward,” received 92% support with 79% strong support. Another 86% agreed that rules should be tightened which allow backup diesel generators for data centers to limit their use during “true emergencies.”</p><p>And a whopping 97% said data centers should be required to report their water sourcing and usage. That’s about as close as you can possibly get to unanimous in a poll.</p><p>New technologies and the companies behind them are quite unpopular, but not as much as you might expect after seeing those numbers above.</p><p>Thirty-nine percent of respondents said they had a favorable view of artificial intelligence (25% “very favorable”), while 51% had an unfavorable view (25% “very unfavorable”).</p><p>Thirty-five percent rated “big tech companies” favorably, while 55% rated them unfavorably.</p><p>And just 30% rated data centers favorably, while 51% rated them unfavorably (32% “very unfavorably,” which was the highest of all three).</p><p>The House held a hearing on the chamber’s version of the POWER Act earlier this month. The Senate held a marathon hearing over two days the same week on a large number of bills dealing with artificial intelligence (which uses lots of data centers), social media, etc.</p><p>Many of these bills appear to come from a desire to address the all too obvious anger of the electorate. Some lobbyists are hoping that the usual legislative “wait and see” inertia sets in. And, if the poll is accurate, voters have not made this a huge priority when it comes to their own state legislators.</p><p>According to the poll, just 37% said they’d view their legislators more favorably if their members supported the bill, while 17% said they’d view them less favorably. Almost half, 48%, said it made no difference either way.</p><p><i>Rich Miller also publishes Capitol Fax, a daily political newsletter, and CapitolFax.com.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-760000" name="module-760000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Rich Miller

entries.title: Dear Abby: In-laws make themselves at home long before dinner
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> How should I handle in-laws who show up three or more hours early for dinner and just sit at the kitchen counter? I have things to do; I don't have time to entertain them, and I can't persuade them to leave the kitchen. It really puts a damper on my final prep and last-minute cleaning. I don't want to be rude. They are nice people, but I can't handle this anymore. — <i>ON DISPLAY IN ILLINOIS</i></p><p><b>DEAR ON DISPLAY:</b> Not everyone likes to have company in their kitchen while they are getting ready to entertain. Does your husband know you feel so strongly about this? If he doesn't, let him know, because you may need his help to get the message across to his folks and siblings that you (BOTH) prefer they don't show up early. Of course, the alternative would be for him to entertain them while you prep.</p><p>There is a poem on this subject in my "Keepers" booklet. It was written years ago by a woman named Susan Sawyer and has appeared in my column. It is aptly titled "Stay Out of My Kitchen."</p><p>Please stay away from my kitchen</p><p>From my dishwashing, cooking and such;</p><p>You were kind to have offered to pitch in</p><p>But thanks, no, thank you so much!</p><p>Please don't think me ungracious</p><p>When I ask that you leave me alone;</p><p>For my kitchen's not any too spacious</p><p>And my routine is strictly my own.</p><p>Tell you what: You stay out of my kitchen</p><p>With its sodden, hot, lackluster lures —</p><p>When you're here, stay out of my kitchen</p><p>And I promise to stay out of yours!</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I am a sales associate at a business that sells sewing machines, vacuums and quilting fabric. I was hired to sell machines. Another employee, "Natasha," was hired to manage the fabric department. In crunch times, our duties overlap. We are paid hourly as well as a commission on the sewing machines.</p><p>Yesterday, I had an outstanding sales day. The store was packed with customers, and Natasha spent the entire day in the fabric department. This allowed me to focus on the sales floor. At the end of the day, we were both exhausted, and I remarked that I'd had a great sales day, briefly listing the items that I sold.</p><p>Today, the boss mentioned that I should not have bragged about my sales because Natasha was upset that she was stuck in the fabric department and unable to sell or demo any machines. It caught me by surprise. Did I do something wrong? — <i>SALESPERSON IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR SALESPERSON:</b> Yes, you did. Your boss told you so. It's no wonder hearing you crow about your good fortune upset Natasha after a long, hard day. In the future, keep your lip zipped (or someone may sew it shut for you).</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Loyola University's lab turns used cooking oil into fuel to run buses
entries.summary: <p>Each weekday, buses running on fuel made of used cooking oil transport thousands of Loyola University Chicago students and staff between its north and Downtown campuses.</p><p>The environmentally-friendly biodiesel, produced at Loyola’s Searle Biodiesel Lab in Rogers Park, is made of vegetable oil instead of fossil fuels. The process also recycles waste from Chicago area restaurants, museums and other sources, including Loyola’s dining halls.</p><p>And anyone can drop off used cooking oil at the lab. </p><p>Zach Waickman, who runs the lab as its senior program manager, pointed out jugs and bottles on a pallet.</p><p>“Maybe someone deep fried a turkey at Thanksgiving. We’ll turn the oil into fuel,” Waickman said. “The oil goes to really good use.”</p><p>But don’t bring lard, pan drippings or petroleum products; the lab only recycles vegetable oil. Waickman also advised against dropping off plastic bags full of oil. </p><p>“We get all sorts of weird stuff,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>People can donate used cooking oil to the Seale Biodiesel Lab at Loyola University.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The lab annually produces about 8,000 gallons of biodiesel for Loyola’s eight buses, after processing around 9,000 gallons of used or expired cooking oil.</p><p>Making biodiesel also creates glycerin as a by-product. Each year, the lab uses the glycerin to produce some 1,500 gallons of hand soap found in all of Loyola’s restrooms. During the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic, the lab also produced hand sanitizer used on campus.</p><p>Using biodiesel benefits air quality and helps reduce global warming, Waickman said.</p><p>“Burning biodiesel releases less particulate matter — that plume of black soot coming from trucks and buses. Particulate matter is filtered out by our lungs and can lead to different breathing issues,” he said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/09/05/illinois-soybean-king-biodiesel-fuel-farmers-chicago-park-district-trucks" target="_blank" >Biodiesel</a> emits 50% to 80% less carbon than regular diesel. It can be made from soybean oil and animal fat, as well as used cooking oil. One advantage of biodiesel is that liquid fuels have more power to operate heavy vehicles like buses and run longer than electric batteries.</p><h3>‘Fueled by fries’</h3><p>The biodiesel program grew out of an undergraduate class project in 2007. It’s just one initiative that has sprouted from Loyola’s Solutions to Environmental Problems course. Waickman was a student in the original class. Over the years, the course has sparked a campus-wide bottled water ban, the Loyola farmers market<b> </b>and campus apiaries.</p><p>The Searle Biodiesel Lab was built in 2013. Two years later, it won an Environmental Protection Agency award as a Safer Choice Partner of the Year.</p><p>The facility houses a biodiesel processor, methanol recovery system and soap production equipment, as well as laboratory space for research and quality testing. Eight to 10 Loyola students work part-time there.</p><p>On a spring afternoon last week, a student in a white lab coat checked the biodiesel system’s tanks and colorful pipes. In a nearby vat, machinery stirred glycerin to make soap. A sign on a wall read: “Loyola: Fueled by fries.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-430000" name="image-430000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb53310/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2F36%2F6e4f35ff42cd8f5548afc6bb9e97%2Floyolaoil-260419-028.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d93082/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2F36%2F6e4f35ff42cd8f5548afc6bb9e97%2Floyolaoil-260419-028.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A sign on the wall of the Seale Biodiesel Lab reads “Loyola: Fueled by Fries.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The lab’s work “is a great example of a zero-waste production process,” Waickman said. Used cooking oil and glycerin “make additional value-add products the university needs for operations.”</p><p>Aiden Geraty, a junior at Loyola, has worked at the lab since October. He rides the university’s buses knowing he helps make them run. And washing his hands in Loyola’s bathrooms is a constant reminder that thousands use the soap he helped make.</p><p>“It’s pretty gratifying that the work I’m doing is being used in a helpful way,” Geraty said.</p><p>Over the years, universities and high schools across the U.S. have sought the lab’s advice on similar projects.</p><p>“There are lots of ways to create high-impact learning opportunities for students that also help build and run the sustainability infrastructure of a school,” Waickman said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/be4f48d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2Fcb%2F11234b2c4a7aaf8cb06a75b9c926%2Floyolaoil-260419-003.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f059cf9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2Fcb%2F11234b2c4a7aaf8cb06a75b9c926%2Floyolaoil-260419-003.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/be4f48d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2Fcb%2F11234b2c4a7aaf8cb06a75b9c926%2Floyolaoil-260419-003.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Zach Waickman (right) speaks with Loyola student Aiden Geraty, who works in the Seale Biodiesel Lab.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A valuable commodity</h3><p>Fuel prices are in the headlines as oil prices spike after the U.S. and Israel started a war on Iran in February. Since then, Iran has been effectively controlling the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for global oil shipping.</p><p>The price of Brent crude oil, the international standard, has swung dramatically, rising from roughly $70 per barrel before the war in late February to more than $119 at times.</p><p>Global affairs regularly shine a brighter spotlight on fossil fuels and alternatives to using them. Soaring gas prices were top of mind when Russia started its war on Ukraine and when the COVID-19 pandemic snarled supply chains.</p><p>But no matter what is happening in the world, environmental sustainability has been a constant at Searle Biodiesel Lab. It’s also financially sustainable; revenues from biodiesel and soap fund its operations. Equipment and research are funded by grants and donors.</p><p>In addition, the lab’s work is an important part of the university’s larger plans. In 2024, Loyola became the first carbon neutral university in the Chicago area. Its 2015 Climate Action Plan aimed to achieve carbon neutrality by 2025.</p><p>The lab is part of a local network that supports sustainability. It buys used cooking oil from Green Grease Environmental, based in Burnham, near Indiana.</p><p>Green Grease collects oil in special trucks from about 1,000 local businesses and institutions such as the Art Institute of Chicago and the Shedd Aquarium. It cleans and filters the used cooking oil so it can be turned into biodiesel by Loyola and other facilities.</p><p>Since 2012, Green Grease has recycled more than 7 million gallons of used cooking oil. Business has grown and the company expects to recycle 1 million gallons this year.</p><p>Green Grease owner Tony Demma said the company is a “triple bottom line” enterprise that makes money while benefiting the environment and creating social impact.</p><p>It’s based in Burnham “to help revitalize the area and provide good paying jobs for the local community,” Demma said. Green Grease has about 15 employees.</p><p>At the lab, students test for contaminants like water, sediment and free fatty acids. The oil is processed for a couple weeks<b> </b>in closed tanks.</p><p>Then the lab sells its biodiesel to Al Warren Oil, the company running the depot that operates Loyola’s shuttles. The university’s buses run on an average blend of 20% biodiesel mixed with diesel.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f60cb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7674x4307+0+406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F4d%2Ff7bd821b48c29bd0de7ed0cc0005%2Floyolaoil-260419-034.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f60cb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7674x4307+0+406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F4d%2Ff7bd821b48c29bd0de7ed0cc0005%2Floyolaoil-260419-034.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/199db31/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7674x4307+0+406/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F4d%2Ff7bd821b48c29bd0de7ed0cc0005%2Floyolaoil-260419-034.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f60cb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7674x4307+0+406/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F4d%2Ff7bd821b48c29bd0de7ed0cc0005%2Floyolaoil-260419-034.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Loyola University Chicago’s shuttle buses run on biodiesel made from used cooking oil produced at the Seale Biodiesel Lab in the School of Environmental Sustainability. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Over the years, biodiesel technology has improved and disproved misconceptions about the fuel’s performance. Biodiesel demand has grown in the U.S., due to favorable government policies and other factors.</p><p>In 2024, 5.9 billion gallons of biodiesel were produced in the U.S., compared to 3 billion gallons in 2021, according to Clean Fuels Alliance America. Biodiesel has potential for many kinds of transportation, including freight and shipping.</p><p>Used cooking oil has also become a valuable commodity. In 2020, CME Group, parent of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, began trading used cooking oil futures contracts. The global used cooking oil market is projected to grow from $7.4 billion in 2024 to $12.2 billion by 2034, according to Global Market Insights, due to growing biodiesel demand, government initiatives and other reasons.</p><p>Back at Loyola, Geraty finished mixing chemicals at a lab bench crowded with test tubes and equipment. He acknowledged that biodiesel and soap won’t change the world on their own.</p><p>“But when you apply an environmental mindset, it can have an impact,” Garity said. “It’s part of a larger effort to be good stewards of the environment.”</p><p><i>Contributing: AP</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/09/05/illinois-soybean-king-biodiesel-fuel-farmers-chicago-park-district-trucks" >In Illinois, soybean is king and helps fuel everything from farmers to Chicago Park District trucks</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: How we photographed Oscar-winning Tarell Alvin McCraney
entries.summary: <p>This week’s top photo comes from Visual Journalist <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/anthony-vazquez" target="_blank" >Anthony Vazquez</a>. Follow him on <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/avazquezphoto?utm_source=ig_web_button_share_sheet&igsh=ZDNlZDc0MzIxNw==" target="_blank" >Instagram</a>.</p><h3><b>What makes this photo important?</b></h3><p>Tarell Alvin McCraney, playwright and Steppenwolf Theatre Ensemble member, co-wrote the Oscar-winning film, "Moonlight," which was based on his unreleased semi-autobiography, “In Moonlight Black Boys Look Blue." Now, his work in "Windfall" centers on police violence, money and religion. </p><p>This image is important because it places McCraney directly within the immersive world of his creation. By integrating the subject with the symbolic signage and production elements of the stage, the photo goes beyond a standard portrait and serves as a visual bridge between the artist and his work. It depicts the scale of the theater while highlighting the creative process and the themes of cultural relevance that define the production. It also gives the reader a sense of scale and production they can expect when they go see the play.</p><h3><b>How did you get the photo?</b></h3><p>The primary objective of this assignment was to photograph the theatrical essence of the environment. Since Tarell Alvin McCraney is a playwright, we wanted the image to showcase the world he created. Fortunately, we were able to use the stage as our primary backdrop. Every visual element, including the protest signs, the scenery and the stage itself is a functional component of the production. I used a two-point lighting setup with strobes and umbrellas on stands on either side of him to balance the exposure of the foreground with the background. This helped eliminate harsh shadows on McCraney and props on the ground. This allowed the ambient light of the theater and the seating to fill the frame, which added depth to the image and successfully showcased the scale of the theater.</p><p><b>Technical details:</b><br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-9460a7e0-1f16-11f1-ad7a-f35fc19f6d46" style="font-style: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; text-decoration-line: none; text-decoration-thickness: auto; text-decoration-style: solid; background-repeat: no-repeat; box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0px 0px 0px 10px; padding: 0px 0px 0px 20px; list-style: outside; caret-color: rgb(42, 42, 42); color: rgb(42, 42, 42); font-family: Arial; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: -0.018px; padding-inline-start: 48px;"><li>Equipment: Canon EOS R5 with an RF 28-70mm f/2L lens</li><li>Focal length: 24mm</li><li>Aperture: f/2.8</li><li>Exposure: 1/160</li><li>ISO: 1600</li><li>Lighting: Two Godox 860 II with umbrellas</li></ul><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/10/windfall-steppenwolf-play-tarell-alvin-mccraney-moonlight-writer" >In ‘Windfall,’ Oscar winner Tarell Alvin McCraney asks if money really buys justice for Black men</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h3>14 more photos from Chicago Public Media photojournalists:</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"
