entries.title: Pope Leo XIV channels Cardinal Bernardin's teachings to stand against Iran war
entries.summary: <p>I came to the <a class="Link" href="https://ctu.edu/" target="_blank" >Catholic Theological Union</a> in September 2024 on a scholarship, not as a theologian but as a civil engineer pulled toward questions of justice. One course I did not expect to stay with me was taught by <a class="Link" href="https://ctu.edu/faculty/steven-millies/#dipipopup-25809343#popup_on_load" target="_blank" >Steven Millies</a> — a semester-long examination of Cardinal Joseph Bernardin, Chicago's most beloved archbishop. Now, watching Pope Leo XIV <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2026/04/13/pope-leo-president-trump-attack-iran-war-chicago-catholics-reaction" target="_blank" >rebuke</a> President Donald Trump over the war in Iran, I keep returning to what I learned in that room.</p><p>This confrontation is not random. The pope, born Robert Prevost, <a class="Link" href="https://ctu.edu/ctu_alumni/bishop-robert-francis-prevost-osa/" target="_blank" >graduated from CTU in 1982</a>. He sat in classrooms shaped by the same tradition I have been studying. That tradition has a name — the consistent ethic of life.</p><p>Bernardin developed this idea in the 1980s to address fragmented moral discourse in American public life. His argument was simple and demanding: Human life forms one seamless thread. You cannot protect it in one place and ignore it in another. </p><p>Poverty, war, capital punishment, the treatment of migrants are not separate issues filed under different political parties. They are expressions of a single question: Do we take human life seriously, everywhere, without exception? In Chicago, he built interfaith coalitions across racial and neighborhood lines, insisting that a city holds together only when no thread is left behind.</p><p>When Pope Leo XIV <a class="Link" href="https://www.vaticannews.va/en/pope/news/2026-03/pope-leo-xiv-celebrates-palm-sunday-mass-rome.html" target="_blank" >declared on Palm Sunday</a> that God "does not listen to the prayers of those who wage war," he was not freelancing. When he described threats against Iranian infrastructure as <a class="Link" href="https://www.reuters.com/world/europe/pope-leo-calls-trumps-threat-against-iran-truly-unacceptable-2026-04-07/" target="_blank" >“truly unacceptable,”</a> he was applying Bernardin's logic: A civilization is a seamless garment too. You cannot claim to honor life while threatening to destroy a people's capacity to live it.</p><p>The Chicago that shaped Bernardin also shaped CTU. The school sits at the intersection of Catholic social teaching and the lived realities of a working city. That is not an abstraction. It is a curriculum.</p><p>What we are watching is not a personal feud between a pope from Chicago and a president from Queens. It is a collision between two answers to the same question: What does power owe to human life?</p><p>I came to Chicago as an engineer, trained to build systems that hold. What I found here was a tradition that insists those systems must hold for everyone, or they do not hold at all. Right now, from the Vatican, that tradition is speaking in the accent of this city.</p><p><i>Yunus Emre Tozal, graduate student, Catholic Theological Union</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-350000" name="module-350000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>I’m a believer </h3><p>I attended Catholic grammar and high school and was an altar boy. As I grew older, I became a lapsed Catholic. With Pope Leo XIV standing up to Donald Trump, maybe it’s time to stand with Leo and return to the church.</p><p><i>Bob Rosinia, Lincoln Square</i></p><h3>Troublesome twosome </h3><p>"I certainly think that in some cases, it would be best for the Vatican to stick to matters of morality, to stick to matters of what’s going on with the Catholic Church, and let the president of the United States stick to dictating American public policy," Vice President <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/policy/international/5833285-pope-leo-trump-clash/" target="_blank" >JD Vance said</a>.</p><p>There is so<i> </i>much wrong with Vance's statement, starting with his implied belief that there was no moral aspect to attacking Iran, but not necessarily ending with his claim that the president "dictates" public policy.</p><p>Presidents do not dictate policy. Dictators do. However, I do appreciate the vice president's candor about what Donald Trump is trying to be, although I doubt that he intended to be that<i> </i>candid.</p><p>As for his claim that Trump took down a Christlike image of himself "because he realized a lot of people weren’t understanding his humor," Trump makes a lot of crude and insensitive attempts at being funny, but years of observation have led to my doubt that he has a genuinely humor-capable bone in his body.</p><p><i>Curt Fredrikson, Mokena</i></p><h3><b>Expand mail-in ballot drop-off sites</b></h3><p>Illinois has long been a national leader in protecting both voter access and election security. Our state has demonstrated that expanding election participation and maintaining election integrity are not competing goals — they are complementary pillars of a healthy democracy.</p><p>Today, however, Illinois voters face new uncertainty that requires proactive action from state lawmakers.</p><p>The League of Women Voters of Illinois is urging the General Assembly to update Illinois election law to allow voters to return completed vote-by-mail ballots — sealed in their official return envelopes — at any polling place within their election authority’s jurisdiction on Election Day.</p><p>This change is simple, practical and urgently needed.</p><p>Recent developments at the federal level, including the possibility that the U.S. Supreme Court could prohibit states from counting vote-by-mail ballots received after Election Day — even when postmarked on time — threaten to invalidate thousands of lawful votes nationwide. Illinois allows ballots postmarked by Election Day to be counted if they arrive within a statutory grace period. If federal action removes that protection, voters who followed every rule could still see their ballots rejected due solely to postal delays beyond their control.</p><p>No eligible voter should lose their voice because of the speed of the mail.</p><p>Many Illinois residents rely on vote by mail — including seniors, voters with disabilities, caregivers and workers whose schedules make in-person voting difficult. While election authorities across Illinois provide a few secure drop boxes, those locations are limited in number and not easily accessible for many voters. By contrast, polling places are widely distributed, familiar to voters and staffed by trained election judges on Election Day.</p><p>The League of Women Voters of Illinois is advocating for legislation this session to allow vote-by-mail ballot drop-off at polling places within a voter’s election authority jurisdiction statewide, up to and including on Election Day, so the option is available in time for the November election.</p><p><i>Becky Simon, president, League of Women Voters of Illinois</i></p><h3>Standing with Latina survivors </h3><p>As I read the recent news stories about César Chávez, my heart breaks for the survivors who endured years of abuse and for Dolores Huerta, one of my role models.</p><p>As I reflect on these painful stories, I reflect on my own story of sexual abuse as a little girl, my own silence, and the stories of other Latinas who have also experienced abuse at the hands of a father, brother, tío, cousin, family friend, etc.</p><p>Recent headlines have focused on shock and disappointment, specifically referencing Chávez’s tarnished legacy. The truth is, I am not shocked, and I would bet many other Latinas aren’t either.</p><p>Latinas who experience abuse are often silenced. The “Me Too” movement calls for women to stand in solidarity and refuse silence. For some reason, one that I could not pinpoint until now, this movement has always felt distant to me. </p><p>Amid the breaking news about Chávez, I have found myself reflecting on my own silence and that of so many Latinas. For Latinas, barriers to speaking out are cultural and systemic, which are rooted in machismo, a system of dominance reinforcing that women should endure harm to preserve family and a man’s reputation.</p><p>The sexual abuse perpetrated by Chávez is not an exception.</p><p>This moment is an opportunity to confront a larger truth: Latinas often experience abuse that is hidden, excused or ignored. Latinas are silenced not only by those who harm them, but by the communities around them, by the people they confide in, by those who witness the signs and say nothing.</p><p>Research shows that approximately 1 in 6 Latinas in the U.S. will experience sexual violence in their lifetime. Those numbers do not show the full picture. They do not account for women who do not feel empowered to speak.</p><p>This moment calls for something deeper than disappointment. It calls for the Latino community to look inward. Real change requires our community to stand with survivors loudly, to amplify their voices even when it feels uncomfortable and to insist on accountability. Change begins when we are willing to risk discomfort, disrupt tradition, break generational cycles and say enough is enough.</p><p><i>Alexis Hernandez,</i> <i>Pilsen</i> </p><h3>Flooding in Chicago is natural </h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" >Flooding in Chicago</a> has been a concern for years. Chicago sits on a marsh — quite a large one, with the South and West sides almost entirely composed of swampland. To suggest that those areas are somehow more affected by flooding because they are poorer ignores the base geography.</p><p>Man, in his never-ending quest for riches, determined that swampland could be converted into a money-making enterprise if drained, and entered into a decades-long project to make it so. What seemed like a good idea at the time, draining swamps to create marketable, e.g. moneymaking, land, did not appear to take the purpose of the land into account.</p><p>Wetlands exist for a reason. To prevent flooding, stabilize aquifers and provide a habitat for wildlife. They had been thus for hundreds or even thousands of years.</p><p>We made our choice, and it was enterprise over nature. Now, we pay the price. Spending funds on projects to divert the water when it comes and constantly repairing the damage from floods does nothing to address the cause. Until and unless considerably more emphasis is placed on creating green spaces and working with the land instead of against it, Chicago, like other parts of the Midwest that foolishly drained swamps to create usable land, will be spinning their wheels in the fight against flooding.</p><p>It appears that Mother Nature is having the last laugh.</p><p><i>Barbara Orze, Cedar Lake, Indiana</i></p><h3><b>Make public health a priority</b></h3><p>As the Sun-Times' Elvia Malagón recently <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/31/hiv-aids-infections-up-in-chicago-after-years-of-decline-as-cases-rise-among-latinos" target="_blank" >reported</a>, cuts to federal public health dollars for HIV response worsen health risks.</p><p>In this wealthy country, we can — and indeed, we should — fully fund public health.</p><p>Different from medicine, public health is our collective response to collective health problems. Any health problem requires both health care for individuals and a public health approach focused on preventing disease, reducing risks and responding with consideration for populations. Public health and medicine must work together, which requires sufficient funding.</p><p>Congressional Republicans and President Donald Trump are cutting much more than public funding for HIV response — a proposal to cut Centers for Disease Control and Prevention <a class="Link" href="https://www.tfah.org/report-details/funding-report-2025/" target="_blank" >funding by 53%</a> demonstrates their disdain for Americans' well-being.</p><p>The long-standing neglect-boom-bust-neglect public health funding cycle is exemplified by the too-little, too-late COVID-19 temporary funding influx and later reduction. Elected officials unreasonably expect health departments to build a plane while flying it after denying necessary resources before crises hit.</p><p>Dedicated public health workers do their best to address problems despite these untenable conditions. Yet, many talented public health workers leave their jobs because the funding for their positions gets cut or the expectations are too high with the limited resources. That is a recipe for burnout and talent loss, and yes, unmet public health needs.</p><p>Preventable sicknesses, injuries and early deaths occur when public health staffing and infrastructure are underfunded. We need robust, flexible and sustained public health funding to maintain capacity.</p><p>We must not hold our breath for the Trump regime and his Republican underlings. But we are not helpless here in the Chicago area and Illinois. Our elected leaders must respond, and we who value community health should demand that they find the money to fund public health.</p><p><i>Wesley Epplin, policy director, Health & Medicine Policy Research Group</i></p><h3>Tax thoughts </h3><p>After all these years of denigrating former Mayor Rahm Emanuel, I find myself in agreement on one thing: <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/04/15/rahm-emanuel-online-betting-prediction-markets-tax-proposal-presidential-bid" >taxing sports betting to fund science and technology</a>. But let’s not stop there — use those taxes to also fund the rest of STEAM education: engineering, arts and mathematics. And those billionaires or millionaires who don’t already follow MacKenzie Scott’s fabulous lead (notably donating to the Girl Scouts and universities), tax them too!</p><p><i>Maja Ramirez, Avondale</i> </p><h3></h3>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Blackhawks players set playoffs as 2027 goal, but Kyle Davidson knows that'll require enormous leap
entries.summary: <p>Kyle Davidson has learned not to set specific expectations for specific seasons.</p><p>Two years ago, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> general manager made that mistake while looking ahead to 2024-25, declaring: "It's time to take a step forward. Previous to this year, we finished third-last. This year, we finished second-last. That can’t happen again."</p><p>The Hawks, in fact, did finish second-last again. Then they finished second-last again in the just-concluded 2025-26 season.</p><p>Screenshots of that Davidson quote were re-circulated this week by the impatient portion of the Hawks' fan base, citing it as evidence of Davidson failing his own goals and not deserving his new contract extension.</p><p>Davidson's job security remains strong, despite the fan frustration. But he avoided setting the same trap for himself during his end-of-season news conference Thursday, using vaguer language while still expressing a general need for improvement.</p><p>(Davidson can also point to points, which have increased from 52 to 61 to 72 over the last three seasons, as evidence that steps forward actually have been taken, just not reflected in the standings.)</p><p>Hawks players, however, did not exercise the same caution. They were blunt — and ambitious — with their goals for 2026-27.</p><p>"Going into next season, playoffs are the expectation," Alex Vlasic said. "We're not here to do this again. And we all know that in the locker room. We have confidence in our group. Going into the summer, we're all excited about coming back here next year and really making a push."</p><p>Player after player stood at a podium and uttered similar versions of that statement.</p><p>"This kind of has to be the last year of...not playing until the end of the year, [not] playing for something in the playoff standings," <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/16/connor-bedard-blackhawks-captain-ready-leadership-alex-vlasic" target="_blank" >Connor Bedard</a> said.</p><p>"You saw that for the first half of the year. We were in that spot. And then...we slid a little bit. As my production went down, I feel responsible for that, which adds motivation. I think all our guys are ready to make that push."</p><p>Indeed, the Hawks were in a spot on Nov. 18 this season after their 10-5-4 start, and they briefly pulled back within three points of a spot on Jan. 10 (while Bedard was injured). That feels like a different universe now, though.</p><p>"We're ready to take that step," Ryan Donato said. "The difference is really small, but the work you have to put in [to overcome] that difference is really big. Guys know that and are hungry and excited to prove that next season."</p><p>Said Spencer Knight: "I hope we continue to grow and our mindset...starts to be, 'Hey, we're not just trying to be a collective group of players in the NHL. We're trying to be a legitimate team in this league.' That's probably going to start next year."</p><p>Players expressed the same things in their exit interviews with Davidson, which he enjoyed hearing.</p><p>"We've gone through these the last number of years where it's a little more skeptical," Davidson said. "This felt different. This felt a lot more excitement and positivity."</p><p>Personally, he seems skeptical about a playoffs-or-bust mindset. The Hawks just finished 18 points out in a weak year for the Western Conference. They would likely need to improve by 20-plus points next season to squeeze in.</p><p>But the fans <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/16/blackhawks-attendance-ticket-sales-centennial-rewarding-fans" target="_blank" >deserve</a> competitive hockey, and objectively it is Davidson's job to give it to them as soon as possible. He would be thrilled by a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/12/blackhawks-vision-next-season-summer-improvement-connor-bedard-ryan-donato" target="_blank" >breakthrough</a> next season.</p><p>"The players are very motivated to make sure that we're pushing for the playoffs next year," Davidson said. "That is a goal we believe they should come in a training camp trying to achieve. </p><p>"It's something that would require a major step forward. But the group is motivated and talented enough that we feel it's not unrealistic to expect that."</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: As Chicago's indie venues struggle, local musicians face their own financial burdens
entries.summary: <p>Ava Brennan is a singer-songwriter who plays live a few times per month in Chicago.</p><p>But like most musicians, she can’t survive on her art alone so she also teaches music, works for a nonprofit and takes tickets at the door of a distillery that doubles as a live music venue.</p><p>“We're all having trouble generating revenue, period,” said Brennan, 26, of Lake View.</p><p>On Saturday, she attended a community expo for artists at the Color Club events space in Irving Park. The event was part of <a class="Link" href="https://civlchicago.com/civl-fest" target="_blank" >CIVL Fest</a>, which opened on Thursday and runs through April 25. Hosted by the Chicago Independent Venue League, the citywide celebration features roughly 50 shows across nearly 30 locations.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ava Brennan stands in the workshop space of the “GREEN Community Expo” at Color Club in Irving Park on Saturday, April 18, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Now in its fourth year, the festival occurs at a crucial time in the local music industry. Just one out of four of Chicago’s indie music venues is profitable, due to rising operational costs, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/03/chicago-indie-venues-health-metro-ramova-salt-shed" >according to a recent report</a> by CIVL. Since its release, elected officials have reached out to the advocacy organization and local venues to “continue a conversation,” said CIVL Executive Director Billy Helmkamp. And on April 15, a coalition of 33 states, <a class="Link" href="https://illinoisattorneygeneral.gov/news/story/attorney-general-raoul-coalition-win-trial-against-live-nation-and-ticketmaster" target="_blank" ><u>including Illinois</u></a>, won a lawsuit against Ticketmaster and its parent company, Live Nation. A federal jury <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2026/04/16/nx-s1-5787491/ticketmaster-live-nation-verdict-monopoly-remedies" target="_blank" ><u>declared the latter a monopoly</u></a>, ruling that the corporation had overcharged consumers and eliminated competition in markets across the country.</p><p>In the meantime, Chicago venues are still struggling, and local artists are feeling the financial burden. In addition to working multiple jobs, they are seeking out alternative spaces and advocating for systemic change.</p><p>”It makes me sad because that's my favorite way to experience music,” Brennan said of going to shows at indie spots. “I go to smaller, mid-sized venues where I might even know the owner or the booker, and just feel super comfortable in a space where independent artists are empowered.”</p><p>Helmkamp said CIVL Fest is a “call to action” for the city to invest in the indie ecosystem. And while he praised the Live Nation verdict, he stressed the need for support from local and state government.</p><p>“We still need policies that recognize the value of independent venues and actively help them survive and thrive,” he said. “Independent venues function like infrastructure for culture and jobs and tourism, but venues don't get treated like infrastructure when it comes to protections and incentives or relief.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-360000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Karen Suarez Flint sits at her table at the “GREEN Community Expo” at Color Club in Irving Park on Saturday, April 18, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>That sentiment was shared by Karen Suarez Flint, the secretary-treasurer of the Chicago Federation of Musicians union, which represents about 2,000 artists.</p><p>“Why can't there be a committee of alderpersons working together for our independent venues scene here?” asked Flint, who was a vendor at the community expo on Saturday. “We need to be part of the solution, talking to legislators, being on podcasts. There has to be a way to work together to help the ecosystem thrive so that we can get to the point where musicians are making a little more.”</p><p>For drummer George Rezek, 26, of Lake View, making a living just playing music seems unattainable.</p><p>“Really popular bands still have other jobs,” said Rezek, who also works for the Earth Libraries music label, another vendor at the expo. “The idea of one day buying a house playing music is completely unrealistic and impossible.”</p><p>But it’s not the venues’ fault, he said.</p><p>“If you look at it on a macro scale, the music industry has been squeezed of all funds for a long time,” he said. “Streaming has devalued the music and has caused artists to make less money.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>George Rezek poses for a portrait at the “GREEN Community Expo” at Color Club in Irving Park on Saturday, April 18, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Brennan said attending a workshop by Arts Alliance Illinois at the expo helped her consider the bigger picture.</p><p>“They were getting us to look at it all as a system that's entangled with local government, which I don't think about very often,” she said. “It's important to do some community organizing to advocate for legislation where the arts are being funded.”</p><p>Venues like Color Club are using funding to support local performers. Co-owner Abby Monroe said a $40,000 grant from the Live Music Society nonprofit enabled the center to run a monthly emerging artists showcase called "Green." </p><p>But Color Club must also offer a variety of other arts programming to remain open, Monroe said. And they are currently only breaking even. </p><p>"You can't just do one thing anymore," Monroe said. "You have to be open to what the market is asking for.”</p><p>In addition to playing at Color Club and other music venues, Brennan also seeks out alternative spaces, from vintage clothing stores to record shops. </p><p>“So I can just do a show where it's me and my guitar and an amp and some pedals," she said. "We are making DIY shows happen, which are more well-paid than at a venue, because it might just be somebody's house and they're collecting 10 bucks at the door.”<br></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: Jameson Taillon doesn't discount struggling Mets, then holds them to one run in six innings
entries.summary: <p>It would have been easy to think silly thoughts and let the Mets’ losing streak get into his head. But Cubs right-hander <b>Jameson Taillon</b> wouldn’t allow it on his drive Saturday to Wrigley Field.</p><p>‘‘I wasn’t going to fall into the trap of thinking this was a bad team or a struggling team,’’ Taillon said. ‘‘You look at the names up and down the lineup, I came in here not wanting to be the reason they got hot. I had that conversation with myself.’’</p><p>Taillon yielded one run and five hits, struck out four and walked three in six innings and was the winning pitcher in the Cubs’ 4-2 victory, thanks to a three-run home run by pinch hitter <b>Carson Kelly</b> in the sixth. The performance lowered his ERA to a respectable 3.97.</p><p>‘‘We’re trending in the right direction,’’ Taillon said. ‘‘I’m still not throwing as many strikes as I’d like, and there were some uncompetitive curveballs, stuff like that. But today was a really good step. I thought the stuff was crispy. The four-seam [fastball] had better carry, and the sweeper got better as we went.’’</p><h3>Martin next man up in pen</h3><p>The Cubs selected right-hander <b>Corbin Martin’s</b> contract from Triple-A Iowa to take closer <b>Daniel Palencia’s</b> spot on the active roster. Palencia (strained left oblique) was the 10th Cubs pitcher added to a burgeoning injured list Friday.</p><p>Right-hander <b>Cade Horton</b>, who had the makings of a staff ace, was moved to the 60-day IL to open a spot on the 40-man roster for Martin. Horton had Tommy John surgery Thursday.</p><p>The Cubs signed Martin, 30, to a minor-league deal for depth during the offseason. In parts of four seasons, he has a 6.54 ERA in 74⅔ innings with the Astros, Diamondbacks and Orioles.</p><p>Martin is out of minor-league options and would be exposed to waivers if the Cubs try returning him to Iowa.</p><p>Martin, who had Tommy John surgery in 2020 and surgery to repair a lat tendon in 2023, dealt with back spasms after pitching in Iowa’s opener and was out for two weeks before pitching again Tuesday. He allowed one earned run in two innings.</p><p>He pitched in eight games during spring training, allowing six earned runs in 7⅔ innings with 10 strikeouts and five walks.</p><p>‘‘He pitched two innings in Iowa, so this is need-based,’’ manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said.</p><p><b>Jacob Webb</b>, <b>Ben Brown</b> and Martin are the only right-handers in the bullpen.</p><p>‘‘We’ve had a lot of needs, and we wanted to get a right-hander here, frankly,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘Ideally, he would have pitched more in Iowa after the back injury, but we have a need.’’</p><h3>PCA not moping</h3><p>Center fielder <b>Pete Crow-Armstrong</b> seems to be keeping his frustration over a .218/.265/.282 batting line to himself. Perhaps playing with multiple stars at the World Baseball Classic helped.</p><p>‘‘The WBC was a great experience for Pete in that regard,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘Being around those guys made him understand who he can aspire to be. I see he’s improving at it.’’</p><p>But Counsell said he doesn’t want Crow-Armstrong to be anything other than himself, which is someone who plays with his hair on fire.</p><p>‘‘There is a lot of good in that we don’t want to acknowledge, and that’s sometimes hard to understand,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘He needs to play with emotion, and we should not try to take that out of him. We should try to get it going in the right direction. It’s too much fun to watch.’’</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Julianna Stratton talks about the fight she’s bringing to Washington at Power Rising Summit
entries.summary: <p>In a fireside chat at the Power Rising Summit, held in Chicago, Lt. Governor and Democratic U.S. Senate nominee Juliana Stratton said her current campaign simply reflects voters' aggressive mentality.</p><p>“When I've traveled the state of Illinois, what I've heard from people is that they want to see us bring a real fight to Washington,” Stratton said. “That has been my charge from the people of Illinois.”</p><p>That chat occurred Saturday during the final plenary session on day two of the Summit, a gathering of Black women seeking to harness their collective energy to create an actionable agenda. She was joined by Minyon Moore, co-convener of the Summit and Chair of the 2024 Democratic National Convention.</p><p>Stratton tied her work as a practitioner of restorative justice to the summit’s goal of bringing together a diverse group of women to solve problems that have plagued the Black community for generations.</p><p>“You don't have to go outside the community to solve challenges. Because, as we say in the restorative justice world, the wisdom is in the room,” she said. “If you want to know how to repair that harm through the restorative justice lens, it says that you talk to the people who know their communities.”</p><p>Stratton also took aim at President Trump, vowing to push the Democratic Party to be more “courageous.”</p><p>“There's a need to use my voice and the bully pulpit that I will be given, but there's also a need to push our party — and I'm a Democrat — to be more courageous in this moment, because this is not a normal president, he's not a normal person," Stratton said. "So you don't go about business as usual with somebody who is not normal.”</p><p>When asked what courage looks like, Stratton said it means overcoming the mindset that not much can get done.</p><p>“I've been surprised at how there almost seems to be a ‘go along to get along’ mentality in Washington, D.C.: ‘There's not much we can do. It's so hard,’ and therefore we can't even think big about what's possible,” Stratton said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/81a1790/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f56aa6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4e4f4e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, who is running for senator, speaks during “Power Rising,” a conference for Black women empowerment at Hilton Chicago in the Loop, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As a way forward, Stratton referred to how the Pritzker-Stratton administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2020/2/19/21144038/pritzker-offers-two-budgets-holding-150m-education-graduated-income-tax" target="_blank" ><u>turned</u></a> the state’s finances around after two years without a budget during former Gov. Bruce Rauner’s tenure. She also pointed to their early actions to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2019/2/19/18415653/pritzker-signs-bill-to-increase-minimum-hourly-wage-to-15-by-2025" target="_blank" ><u>raise</u></a> the minimum wage to $15 dollars an hour. The national minimum wage is $7.25 an hour, and hasn’t been raised <a class="Link" href="https://www.dol.gov/agencies/whd/minimum-wage/history" target="_blank" ><u>since</u></a> 2009.</p><p>Stratton said the lack of political will to achieve progressive policies is linked to big money that influences races.</p><p>“There's a strong corporate special interest that often seems to take center stage in Washington. We have to keep the needs of the people at the forefront, which is why I don't take any corporate PAC money in this race,” she said.</p><p>Stratton received a $5 million donation in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/01/31/juliana-stratton-jb-pritzker-campaign-contribution-5-million" target="_blank" ><u>January</u></a> from a pro-Stratton super PAC that was funded by Gov. Pritzker and members of his family. She was also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/13/super-pacs-influence-2026-illinois-primary-races-glossary" target="_blank" ><u>supported</u></a> by the DLGA PAC, the Democratic Lieutenant Governors Association, that spent $222,000 on her behalf.</p><p>Speaking to an audience full of Black women at the Hilton Chicago, Stratton addressed the elephant in the room: Black women’s unemployment. According to the Economic Policy Institute, in <a class="Link" href="https://www.epi.org/blog/black-women-suffered-large-employment-losses-in-2025-particularly-among-college-graduates-and-public-sector-workers/" target="_blank" ><u>2025</u></a>, Black women saw the largest employment losses compared to Black men, AAPI, Latino, and white women. Most losses resulted from federal job cuts, disproportionately affecting college-educated Black women who worked in the public sector.</p><p>In response, she wants to stand up for DEI and restore and protect those jobs that were lost.</p><p>“This administration is trying to make it a bad word to talk about diversity, equity and inclusion, she said. “Not because we can't do the work and always have been able to do it, but because of systemic racism and so many other factors, [those opportunities] have been blocked. And we need the intentionality of making sure we can be in those spaces.”</p><p>Stratton said the racial challenges her father faced inspire her to keep moving forward and bring others with her. Growing up in a small town in Pennsylvania, he couldn’t attend his school trip to the nation’s capitol because the city didn’t have a hotel or restaurant that would serve Black people.</p><p>“I'm not talking about my great-grandfather. I'm not talking about my grandfather. This is my dad,” she said.</p><p>“I think about how he passed the baton to all of us to say that whatever space you find yourself in, go there and don't just focus on yourself. Bring somebody else with you.”</p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: Carson Kelly's pinch-hit, 3-run HR sends Cubs to 4th consecutive victory
entries.summary: <p>Cubs manager Craig Counsell pushed back on the suggestion that pinch-hitting for the left-handed-hitting Moises Ballesteros against a left-hander pitcher risks messing with the 22-year-old’s mind, feelings and development.</p><p>‘‘This is a team,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘And we’re going to put players in positions to have success, and that trumps the other thing.’’</p><p>That was Saturday morning — before Counsell called on catcher Carson Kelly to pinch-hit for Ballesteros against Mets left-hander Brooks Raley with two on and two outs in the sixth inning of a tie game hours later.</p><p>Counsell got the righty-against-lefty matchup he preferred, and Kelly was ready, launching Raley’s first pitch into the bleachers for the second pinch home run of his career to fuel the Cubs to a 4-2 victory and extend their winning streak to four games.</p><p>‘‘Pinch-hitting is a hard thing to do,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘You go up there and say you’re going to be aggressive, and he got a good pitch to hit. You have to get your swings off.’’</p><p>It can be hard to be pinch-hit for, but such is life in the majors, as the sweet-swinging, red-hot Ballesteros is learning fast. Right-handed-hitting Matt Shaw batted for him in the Cubs’ 12-4 victory Friday after Ballesteros had two hits, including a homer. He is batting .381, easily the best average among rookies in the majors. He had another hit Saturday and is 10-for-12 with two homers and a double in his last six games.</p><p>But Kelly, who was staying warm and preparing behind the scenes, was the right man for the job against Raley.</p><p>‘‘Heck of a job managing there,’’ said outfielder Ian Happ, whose sixth homer of the season in the second provided the Cubs’ first run against tough right-hander Freddy Peralta. ‘‘Pinch-hit three-run homer, that’s a good day for the manager.’’</p><p>‘‘There are no training wheels,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘We’re going with the best matchups.’’</p><p>The Cubs (11-9) will go for a series sweep Sunday against the Mets (7-14), who have lost a staggering 10 consecutive games, their longest skid since 2004. The Cubs are winning with contributions up and down the lineup and from a bench Counsell is getting the most out of.</p><p>‘‘Guys are prepared, they know their role and they are expecting to play every day,’’ Kelly said.</p><p>And winning helps everyone — from Rookie of the Year candidates such as Ballesteros to veterans such as Kelly — to buy in.</p><p>‘‘It’s good when you have success doing it,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘That’s No. 1. And it makes everybody feel ready and feel part of it and know they’re going to get their turn at some point to get the big hit.’’</p><p>Right-hander Jameson Taillon yielded one run in six innings, allowing a homer to Mark Vientos in the second. Right-hander Ben Brown gave up an unearned run in two innings of relief, and left-hander Caleb Thielbar struck out two in a perfect ninth, pounding the zone with 11 strikes in 14 pitches to earn the save with closer Daniel Palencia (strained oblique) on the injured list.</p><p>The save was the sixth of Thielbar’s career and second with the Cubs.</p><p>But Kelly, who is batting .340, was the hero, and he extended his on-base streak to 14 games since March 30. He’s batting .372 with two homers, three doubles and nine RBI during that stretch.</p><p>‘‘Just looking for a good pitch to drive there,’’ he said. ‘‘Staying prepared to get that call and make a good swing when the pitch is there. It was cool. Very special.</p><p>‘‘This is a special group, and it’s a culture we’ve developed. We pride ourselves on the next guy up, and we’re here to win baseball games.’’</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Resurgent White Sox bats not hot enough as Athletics walk them off 7-6
entries.summary: <p>WEST SACRAMENTO, Calif. — The Athletics walked off the suddenly hot-hitting <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> 7-6 on Saturday with an 11th-inning sacrifice fly from third baseman <b>Max Muncy</b> off reliever <b>Lucas Sims</b>.</p><p><b>Colson Montgomery</b> got the Sox on the board in the second inning with his fourth home run of the season, and the Sox kept piling on Athletics starter Luis Severino. <b>Everson Pereira</b> blooped a double to right field and came around on <b>Reese McGuire</b>’s single, before Andrew Benintendi thwacked a three-run homer, his first of the season.</p><p>A’s shortstop <b>Jacob Wilson</b> responded with a solo blast off <b>Erick Fedde</b> in the second inning, and <b>Lawrence Butler</b> scored on <b>Jeff McNeil</b>’s sacrifice fly in the third.</p><p>Fedde got into a jam in the fifth and <b>Tyler Soderstrom</b> knocked an RBI single off <b>Sean Newcomb</b>, who gave up another run in the sixth. Fedde gave up three runs on two hits and four walks with three strikeouts over 4 ⅔ innings.</p><p><b>Munetaka Murakami</b> slammed his second mammoth home run in as many days, this one a solo blast in the seventh, but <b>Nick Kurtz</b> tied it up with a no-doubt two-run bomb off <b>Jordan Leasure</b> in the bottom half of the inning.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="et" dir="ltr">🚨MUNETAKA MURAKAMI HOME RUN🚨 <a href="https://t.co/6HkumpBoWa">pic.twitter.com/6HkumpBoWa</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2045624564739150314?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 18, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The teams swapped bases loaded opportunities to no avail from the eighth inning on, before the game-winning sac fly.</p><h3><b>Measuring Mune’s moonshot</b></h3><p><b>Munetaka Murakami</b>’s Statcast-projected 431-foot grand slam sure felt like a lowball estimate in the Sox clubhouse the morning after the rookie slugger’s seventh-inning rocket cleared the batter’s eye in center field <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/17/white-sox-seranthony-dominguez-anthony-kay-athletics" target="_blank" ><u>to seal Friday’s 9-2 win</u></a>.</p><p>“I got more than 431. I don't know who's doing that calculation, but that ball was touched right there,” said manager Will Venable, who categorized it as “a majestic blast.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">114.1 MPH<br>OVER the batter's eye<br><br>Munetaka Murakami's first MLB grand slam was a sight to behold! <a href="https://t.co/wFgHwqsLAg">https://t.co/wFgHwqsLAg</a> <a href="https://t.co/YFlLGxwiCB">pic.twitter.com/YFlLGxwiCB</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2045352938231521432?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 18, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>A similar moonshot at Sutter Health Park last summer by Tigers outfielder <b>Riley Greene</b> was clocked at 471 feet.</p><p>From the Sox’ dugout, starter <b>Davis Martin</b> thought <b>Denzel Clarke</b>’s leisurely strides indicated the A’s center fielder had a beat on the 114-mph laser that Murakami cranked off a 98-mph fastball from <b>Elvis Alvarado</b>.</p><p>Then Davis’ jaw dropped.</p><p>“I looked at our pitching coach [<b>Zach Bove</b>] like, ‘Oh my gosh,’” Martin said. “Guy’s got stupid juice.”</p><h3><b>Acuna’s growing pains</b></h3><p><b>Luisangel Acuna</b> snapped an 0-for-21 skid at the plate with a single and followed up with an RBI double for his first extra-base hit in a Sox uniform during their offensive onslaught Friday on a night that encapsulated the 24-year-old’s potential upside and early struggles.</p><p>Acuna, who has learned center field on the job since arriving from the Mets in the <b>Luis Robert Jr.</b> trade, also lost a fly ball in the lights that bounced for a ground-rule double. But he gamely tracked down a few screaming liners and swiped his fifth bag of the season.</p><p>“He's been grinding, like so many of these guys have, and he's working so hard pre-game to get his swing right, to put himself in a good position out there. To get rewarded is great, and just really nice for him, nice for us,” Venable said. “He's a good player and he can hit.”</p><p>Acuna had a paltry .453 OPS entering play Saturday compared to .720 for Robert, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/18/luis-robert-jr-new-york-mets-chicago-white-sox-mlb-all-star" >hit a double in his return to Chicago on Friday</a> against the Cubs but has scuffled at the plate himself over the last week.</p><h3><b>Roster shuffle</b></h3><p>The Sox claimed journeyman right-hander <b>Osvaldo Bido</b> off waivers from the Braves and transferred <b>Prelander Berroa</b> from the 15-day injured list to the 60-day IL in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/11/erick-fedde-white-sox-return-prelander-berroa" target="_blank" ><u>his recovery from Tommy John surgery</u></a>.</p><p>Bido, 30, joins his fourth team with a 5.13 ERA over 64 career games (28 starts) with 185 strikeouts in 203 ⅔ innings. He gave up seven runs in six relief appearances before being designated for assignment by Atlanta.</p><p>Meanwhile, the Astros claimed outfielder <b>Dustin Harris</b>, whom the Sox DFA’d last week.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Luis Robert Jr. — once the 'next Mike Trout' — just trying to get to 'pretty good' with struggling Mets
entries.summary: <p>Luis Robert Jr. and the Mets will always have March 28, 2026.</p><p>The team’s new center fielder hit his first “Amazins” home run in his new home ballpark that night, and it was a big one — a two-run walk-off golfed out to left against Pirates lefty Hunter Barco in the 11th inning.</p><p>Citi Field exploded with joy. Robert circled the bases as a team of stars — Juan Soto, Francisco Lindor, Bo Bichette, Freddy Peralta — prepared to mob him at home plate. The Mets, a team with World Series aspirations, were 2-0. After nine years in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> organization, the last few a certain kind of purgatory, Robert seemed to have found baseball heaven.</p><p>Great trade, Mets.</p><p>How could you let him go, Sox?</p><p>“His talent is unlimited,” Mets television analyst Ron Darling gushed that night.</p><p>But then … oof.</p><p>A 4-2 loss to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> on Saturday at Wrigley Field was the 10th in a row for the Mets, their longest losing streak since 2004. At 7-14, they’re even worse off than Robert’s old team, the Sox, who are busy trying to avoid a fourth straight 100-loss season.</p><p>As for Robert’s performance, the nicest way to put it is that he has been among the more productive hitters in an injury-depleted lineup that, as of late, couldn’t swing its way out of a wet paper bag with a spicy salami. In truth, Robert has been unremarkable at the plate — hitting .258 with two homers and seven RBI — except for his team-high 12 walks, which the Mets attribute to their insistence that the 28-year-old be more selective than he ever was with the South Siders.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/17/miserable-mets-cautionary-tale-cubs-injuries-wrigley-field-mlb" >Cubs couldn’t fall apart like these miserable Mets … or could they?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/17/cubs-put-closer-daniel-palenica-on-15-day-injured-list-with-oblique-strain" target="_blank" >Cubs get 12-4 win over the Mets, but put closer Daniel Palencia on 15-day IL</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Where has Robert’s All-Star talent gone? The Mets haven’t really seen it yet, much as the Sox didn’t really see it after his 38-homer season in 2023.</p><p>That could be why Robert blew off a couple of ink-stained Chicagoans a day after indicating he’d make time for an interview on Saturday, the middle game of this series.</p><p>At least he looks healthy, as anyone who watched him bust it down the first-base line in the sixth and eighth innings would have noticed. Given the painful history of his right hip and hamstrings, with which Sox fans are all too familiar, that’s saying something.</p><p>“Luis has been great from the moment we acquired him,” Mets president of baseball operations David Stears told the Sun-Times. “He’s really bought into what we’re about. He’s worked very well with our performance staff, our medical staff, our coaching staff, and now it’s about getting him to be a consistently healthy and productive player. We’ve seen stretches over the course of his career where he’s done that.</p><p>“Our goal is keeping him on the field for the entire 162. We feel like if we can do that, he’s going to be pretty good and contribute to a good team.”</p><p>“Pretty good” isn’t exactly “the next Mike Trout,” which is how ex-Sox teammate Eloy Jimenez famously described Robert before the latter player’s rookie season.</p><p>Some still are wowed by Robert’s physical tools. The Mets’ prized rookie left fielder, Carson Benge, is one of them.</p><p>“He’s just different in all aspects of the game,” Benge said. “He can affect it on the bases, hitting a home run, making a diving play to save some runs. He’s just one of the best players I’ve ever been around.”</p><p>Reminded three weeks later of his "unlimited" comment, Darling wasn’t ready to make any revisions.</p><p>“I think he’s been great,” Darling said. “He’s played really hard. His defensive play has been impeccable. He’s been one of the best hitters the Mets have had. I know he was hurt a lot with you guys and there was a lot of criticism, but I see all the boxes are checked so far.”</p><p>Stearns, though, is measured on the subject of Robert. With good reason.</p><p>“I think sometimes expectations for young players can get out of hand and at times can even do them a disservice,” he said. “I think in Luis’ case, he’s got a tremendous set of skills, he’s a very talented athlete, and sometimes it just takes time to put all of that together.”</p><p>Robert has been a Gold Glove winner, a rookie of the year runner-up, an All-Star. For a while there, he made the Sox look quite good for giving him a $26 million signing bonus in 2017, the second-highest figure for an international amateur at the time. He certainly has the upside to make the Mets look pretty good eventually.</p><p>These days, they’re hitting so little and losing so much that the folks at Citi Field won’t have much use for any of the Mets, Robert included. The boos there upon the team’s return from this disaster of a road trip could really be something to behold.</p><p>The way Darling figures it, even boos are better than what Robert tended to hear at Rate Field. Silence. Apathy. Echos off empty seats. You get the idea.</p><p>“Booing or cheering, it feels good because you’re excited to be at the ballpark,” Darling said.</p><p>After a hitless streak spanning 17 at-bats, Robert lined a double to left in his final at-bat on Friday. His first two times up Saturday, he doubled and singled off Cubs starter Jameson Taillon, hitting the ball with authority both times.</p><p>Maybe he’s beginning to heat up?</p><p>If not, it could be another very long year.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">LUIS ROBERT JR. WALK-OFF HOME RUN 🍎 <a href="https://t.co/3kYwV3BTav">pic.twitter.com/3kYwV3BTav</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2038037020740567157?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 28, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: WNBA season preview: Breaking down the league into four tiers
entries.summary: <p>Now that WNBA teams have mostly settled their rosters, it’s time to rank them. The Sun-Times presents a four-tiered system to rank teams ahead of the league's 30th season.</p><p><b>Tier 1: Blessed by roster continuity and star power, Tier 1 teams will be competing for a championship.</b></p><p><b>Aces</b><br>The defending champs have the whole crew on board. A’ja Wilson? Check. Jackie Young? Check. Everyone else? Check. They also apparently felt secure enough to take a flier on Chennedy Carter, who has bounced in and out of the league in recent years. But Sky fans will remember her as one of the most talented guards in the game right now.</p><p><b>Liberty</b><br>The Liberty are looking at a potential starting five of Breanna Stewart, Jonquel Jones, Satou Sabally, Sabrina Ionescu and Betnijah Laney-Hamilton. That’s unfair and should not be permitted for the safety and well-being of other teams. To rub salt in the wound, they’ll still bring two excellent guards — Leonie Fiebich and Marine Johannes — off the bench.</p><p><b>Dream</b><br>After getting upset in the first round of the playoffs last year, coach Karl Smesko wanted to run it back. So they locked up Allisha Gray, Rhyne Howard, Brionna Jones, Jordin Canada and Naz Hillmon on multiyear deals, then broke hearts in Chicago by adding Angel Reese. Maybe not quite worthy of the Dream Team nickname, but they’re not too far off, either.</p><p><b>Tier 2: These teams have a lot going for them and probably see themselves as Tier 1 contenders. </b>But they still need to address a few chinks in their armor. </p><p><b>Lynx</b><br>The Lynx have two problems. First and foremost, Napheesa Collier is injured and has yet to announce when she’ll return. Second, they were ravaged by the expansion draft and free agency, losing Bridget Carleton, Alanna Smith and Jessica Shepard. They did retain All-Stars Kayla McBride and Courtney Williams, and snagged a generational passer in Olivia Miles in the draft. But that group won’t scare anyone until Collier is back.</p><p><b>Fever</b><br>What the Fever really needed to do was add a stretch big in free agency. They couldn’t quite make it happen — Azurá Stevens headed to Chicago and Alanna Smith signed with the Wings. Still, they retained their Big Three of Caitlin Clark, Kelsey Mitchell and Aliyah Boston, and added forward Monique Billings. Clark and Billings were cooking in the pick-and-roll during the FIBA World Cup qualifiers.</p><p><b>Mercury</b><br>The 2025 runners-up fell to Tier 2 because they lost Satou Sabally to the Liberty. They also lost two key rotation players, Kitija Laksa and Kathryn Westbeld, in the expansion draft. Will a Big Two of Alyssa Thomas and Kahleah Copper be enough to keep them in championship contention? Doubtful.</p><p><b>Tier 3: These teams will be fighting for a playoff spot. </b>Historically, WNBA teams didn’t even have to be that good to make the postseason, since eight of 12 got in. Now that expansion has grown the league to 15 teams, making the playoffs will actually be an accomplishment.</p><p><b>Sparks</b><br>Kelsey Plum is at the peak of her powers, and the Sparks made solid moves around her, bringing in Nneka Ogwumike and Ariel Atkins in the offseason. Plus, Cameron Brink will be fully healthy for the first time since being drafted in 2024.</p><p><b>Wings</b><br>The Wings fixed their frontcourt by raiding the Lynx, grabbing Alanna Smith and Jessica Shepard in free agency. Now it’s time to find out if the Paige Bueckers–Arike Ogunbowale guard duo really works.</p><p><b>Valkyries</b><br>Expansion teams aren’t supposed to make the playoffs in their first season, but coach Natalie Nakase proved everyone wrong, and she did it without a marquee name. Now she’s got one in All-Star Gabby Williams.</p><p><b>Sky</b><br>The Sky now have two of the most accomplished point guards in Skylar Diggins and Courtney Vandersloot, and young talent in Rickea Jackson, Kamilla Cardoso and Jacy Sheldon. It may be their best roster since 2023.</p><p><b>Tier 4: These teams appear to be on the outside looking into the playoff picture. </b>But most are building around exciting young players, and some might even surprise.</p><p><b>Tempo</b><br>Can coach Sandy Brondello turn this team into last year’s Valkyries? Marina Mabrey, Brittney Sykes and Nyara Sabally give her some firepower, but the Canadians will be underdogs from the jump.</p><p><b>Storm</b><br>Will Dominique Malonga prove she’s in the same franchise-altering class as Bueckers? The No. 2 pick in the 2025 draft looks ready for true stardom.</p><p><b>Mystics</b><br>Will Lauren Betts fit alongside young pillars Sonia Citron and Kiki Iriafen? Washington now has an army of young talent, though it’s still a little unclear how it all fits together.</p><p><b>Sun (will be Comets in 2027)</b><br>Is Aaliyah Edwards on the cusp of a breakout year? If she takes that step, the Sun will have a nice core of Edwards, Saniya Rivers, Aneesah Morrow and Leïla Lacan to take to Houston.</p><p><b>Fire</b><br>Who will the Fire target in the 2027 draft lottery? Their roster looks thin this year, but could get a major glow-up next season with JuJu Watkins, Hannah Hidalgo and Madison Booker all becoming draft-eligible.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Juliana Stratton talks about the fight she’s bringing to Washington at Power Rising Summit
entries.summary: <p>In a fireside chat at the Power Rising Summit, held in Chicago, Lt. Governor and Democratic U.S. Senate nominee Juliana Stratton said her current campaign simply reflects voters' aggressive mentality.</p><p>“When I've traveled the state of Illinois, what I've heard from people is that they want to see us bring a real fight to Washington,” Stratton said. “That has been my charge from the people of Illinois.”</p><p>That chat occurred Saturday during the final plenary session on day two of the Summit, a gathering of Black women seeking to harness their collective energy to create an actionable agenda. She was joined by Minyon Moore, co-convener of the Summit and Chair of the 2024 Democratic National Convention.</p><p>Stratton tied her work as a practitioner of restorative justice to the summit’s goal of bringing together a diverse group of women to solve problems that have plagued the Black community for generations.</p><p>“You don't have to go outside the community to solve challenges. Because, as we say in the restorative justice world, the wisdom is in the room,” she said. “If you want to know how to repair that harm through the restorative justice lens, it says that you talk to the people who know their communities.”</p><p>Stratton also took aim at President Trump, vowing to push the Democratic Party to be more “courageous.”</p><p>“There's a need to use my voice in the bully pulpit that I will be given, but there's also a need to push our party — and I'm a Democrat — to be more courageous in this moment, because this is not a normal president, he's not a normal person," Stratton said. "So you don't go about business as usual with somebody who is not normal.”</p><p>When asked what courage looks like, Stratton said it means overcoming the mindset that not much can get done.</p><p>“I've been surprised at how there almost seems to be a ‘go along to get along’ mentality in Washington, D.C.: ‘There's not much we can do. It's so hard,’ and therefore we can't even think big about what's possible,” Stratton said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/81a1790/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f56aa6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4e4f4e8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6942dc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb3%2F0b%2F7033d47e46c3b1fb70784c63ed6c%2Fstratton-260419-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, who is running for senator, speaks during “Power Rising,” a conference for Black women empowerment at Hilton Chicago in the Loop, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As a way forward, Stratton referred to how the Pritzker-Stratton administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2020/2/19/21144038/pritzker-offers-two-budgets-holding-150m-education-graduated-income-tax" target="_blank" ><u>turned</u></a> the state’s finances around after two years without a budget during former Gov. Bruce Rauner’s tenure. She also pointed to their early actions to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2019/2/19/18415653/pritzker-signs-bill-to-increase-minimum-hourly-wage-to-15-by-2025" target="_blank" ><u>raise</u></a> the minimum wage to $15 dollars an hour. The national minimum wage is $7.25 an hour, and hasn’t been raised <a class="Link" href="https://www.dol.gov/agencies/whd/minimum-wage/history" target="_blank" ><u>since</u></a> 2009.</p><p>Stratton said the lack of political will to achieve progressive policies is linked to big money that influences races.</p><p>“There's a strong corporate special interest that often seems to take center stage in Washington. We have to keep the needs of the people at the forefront, which is why I don't take any corporate PAC money in this race,” she said.</p><p>Stratton received a $5 million donation in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/01/31/juliana-stratton-jb-pritzker-campaign-contribution-5-million" target="_blank" ><u>January</u></a> from a pro-Stratton super PAC that was funded by Gov. Pritzker and members of his family. She was also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/13/super-pacs-influence-2026-illinois-primary-races-glossary" target="_blank" ><u>supported</u></a> by the DLGA PAC, the Democratic Lieutenant Governors Association, that spent $222,000 on her behalf.</p><p>Speaking to an audience full of Black women at the Hilton Chicago, Stratton addressed the elephant in the room: Black women’s unemployment. According to the Economic Policy Institute, in <a class="Link" href="https://www.epi.org/blog/black-women-suffered-large-employment-losses-in-2025-particularly-among-college-graduates-and-public-sector-workers/" target="_blank" ><u>2025</u></a>, Black women saw the largest employment losses compared to Black men, AAPI, Latino, and white women. Most losses resulted from federal job cuts, disproportionately affecting college-educated Black women who worked in the public sector.</p><p>In response, she wants to stand up for DEI and restore and protect those jobs that were lost.</p><p>“This administration is trying to make it a bad word to talk about diversity, equity and inclusion, she said. “Not because we can't do the work and always have been able to do it, but because of systemic racism and so many other factors, [those opportunities] have been blocked. And we need the intentionality of making sure we can be in those spaces.”</p><p>Stratton said the racial challenges her father faced inspire her to keep moving forward and bring others with her. Growing up in a small town in Pennsylvania, he couldn’t attend his school trip to the nation’s capitol because the city didn’t have a hotel or restaurant that would serve Black people.</p><p>“I'm not talking about my great-grandfather. I'm not talking about my grandfather. This is my dad,” she said.</p><p>“I think about how he passed the baton to all of us to say that whatever space you find yourself in, go there and don't just focus on yourself. Bring somebody else with you.”</p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson
